narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 41.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use to ensure we admit students likely to succeed. This prediction draws from patterns in data about thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and more, focusing on who passed the bar without protected factors like gender or race influencing the outcome. For you specifically, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was the most influential positive factor, as it's very close to the 3.24 average among those who passed, steadily pushing the model's view toward expecting you to succeed rather than fail. Your upper-middle family income bracket also helped in a meaningful way, aligning well with the majority of passers who came from similar backgrounds, which nudged the prediction in a favorable direction. However, your first-year decile ranking of 1 stood out as the key drag, far below the average 6th decile for those who passed, significantly raising the model's concern about your early academic performance and tipping the overall assessment toward failure risk. While your LSAT score of 26.5 and third-year decile of 2 were noted, along with your full-time study plans, they played smaller roles compared to these top three. Overall, your profile shows strengths in recent grades and background that mirror successful students, but that low early decile created a notable gap compared to typical admits who ranked much higher from the start. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many students improve dramatically—but the model prioritizes bar passage signals to protect incoming classes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward the 37+ range seen in passers, strengthening your first-year record through additional undergrad courses or explanations in a new application, and highlighting any upward trends in your third-year performance. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds favorably, as the model responds well to improved early indicators. You're clearly capable, with your GPA and background already setting a solid foundation—keep building on that momentum.
